---
title: "Influence Illusion"
author: "Joshua Perez, Ahmed Durrani, Agastya Singh"
date: today
execute: 
  echo: true
format: 
  dashboard: 
    orientation: rows
    theme: flatly
    nav-buttons: 
      location: top 
    embed-resources: true
---

# Introduction

### 1.1: Topical Overview

**1.1: Topical Overview**

Social media has completely changed the way people build influence, promote products, and shape their personal brands. Instagram, especially, has grown into one of the most powerful platforms for influencers, celebrities, and businesses to reach audiences and make revenue. It began as a simple photo-sharing app and has turned into a massive marketing space where visibility can translate directly into income. Today, influencer marketing is worth billions of dollars, and brands often decide who to partner with based on follower counts and visible engagement numbers. But having a large following doesn’t always mean someone has real influence. In fact, research highlighted by the Keller Center for Research at Baylor University suggests that engagement rate how actively followers interact with content may be a better measure of influence than just the total number of followers.

Both external sources were used for inspiration

([https://kellercenter.hankamer.baylor.edu/news/story/2025/follower-count-vs-engagement-uncovering-best-influencer-strategy](https://kellercenter.hankamer.baylor.edu/news/story/2025/follower-count-vs-engagement-uncovering-best-influencer-strategy?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).

(<https://www.influencer-hero.com/blogs/how-to-spot-fake-instagram-accounts-5-ways-to-identify>).

In this project, we focus on three central big-picture questions:

\
**1) Does a higher follower count actually correspond to higher engagement and measurable influence?\
2) How do accounts labeled as fake or suspicious differ from authentic accounts in terms of structural and engagement metrics?\
3) When combining a broad dataset of Instagram accounts with a dataset of top-tier influencers, what patterns emerge about scale, authenticity, and engagement efficiency?**

To explore these questions, we use two publicly available datasets that we combined in Deliverable 2. The first dataset includes about 1,000 Instagram accounts and provides information about whether each account is labeled as authentic or potentially fake, along with structural details like follower count, number of posts, and number of accounts followed. This dataset is available on GitHub:\
<https://github.com/srfdata/2017-10-instagram-influencers/blob/master/analysis/input/labelled_1000_inclprivate.csv>

The second dataset focuses on the top 200 most-followed Instagram accounts. It includes follower counts as well as engagement-related metrics, giving us insight into how large-scale influencers perform. This dataset is available on Kaggle:\
<https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/syedjaferk/top-200-instagrammers-data-cleaned?resource=download>

By joining these two datasets using usernames, we are able to directly compare everyday accounts with some of the most followed influencers on the platform. Bringing them together allows us to look at how scale, authenticity, and engagement interact within the same analytical framework, rather than studying them in isolation.

This topic feels especially relevant right now. Brands and companies often treat follower count as a quick way to judge credibility and influence. It’s easy to assume that more followers automatically means more impact. But if follower counts don’t strongly relate to engagement, or if fake accounts follow different measurable patterns, then those assumptions might be misleading. By examining how popularity, authenticity, and engagement interact, we’re trying to better understand what “influence” actually looks like beyond just a large number on a profile.

There are also some important ethical and methodological limitations to keep in mind. Since both datasets rely only on publicly available Instagram information, we’re limited to the metrics that Instagram chooses to display. The labels identifying accounts as “fake” are likely based on algorithmic patterns or specific criteria, and those methods aren’t perfect, which means some accounts could be misclassified. The data also represents a snapshot from a particular time period, so it may not fully reflect how Instagram works today. Because of these limitations, we see our findings as exploratory rather than final conclusions. Social media changes quickly, and our analysis captures just one moment within a much larger and constantly evolving digital environment.

![https://images.klipfolio.com/website/public/dc1c7d0f-4f5f-4d7a-84f3-5224d9d4c48f/dashboard-examples-instagram_0.png](data:image/png;base64,){width="554"}

# Data Context

**1.2: Data Context**

To better understand how follower count, engagement, and authenticity interact, we rely on two publicly available datasets. Each dataset provides a different lens into the Instagram system. One captures a broader range of accounts with authenticity labels, while the other focuses specifically on high-profile, top-followed influencers. Together, they allow us to examine both everyday accounts and elite influencers within a larger conversation about what influence actually means.

**Dataset 1: Labeled Instagram Accounts (1000 Accounts)**

Public Source:\
<https://github.com/srfdata/2017-10-instagram-influencers/blob/master/analysis/input/labelled_1000_inclprivate.csv>

Created by:\
SRF Data Team (Swiss Radio and Television), including Timo Grossenbacher

This dataset contains approximately 1,000 Instagram accounts and was compiled in 2017 as part of a broader investigation into influencer authenticity and bot detection. The sample includes both public and private accounts and labels each account as either authentic or potentially fake based on specific criteria. In addition to authenticity labels, the dataset records structural profile characteristics such as follower count, number of accounts followed, number of posts, and whether the account has a profile picture. These features were collected using publicly visible Instagram profile data, likely through scraping and automated data collection methods.

In total, the dataset includes roughly 1,000 account-level observations. The data reflects Instagram’s structure around 2017 and is not limited to a single country, meaning it represents a global mix of users rather than a specific geographic region. A major benefit of this dataset is that it allows us to directly compare accounts labeled as fake versus authentic, which is central to our research questions. However, the sample size is relatively small compared to Instagram’s overall user base, and the authenticity labels may not be perfectly accurate. This means that while the dataset is useful for identifying patterns, it may not fully represent the entire Instagram population.

**Dataset 2: Top 200 Instagrammers (Cleaned)**

Public Source:\
<https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/syedjaferk/top-200-instagrammers-data-cleaned?resource=download>

Created by:\
Syed Jafer 

This dataset contains information on 200 of the most-followed Instagram accounts at the time of collection. The accounts primarily include celebrities, major influencers, and public figures with extremely large audiences. The data was scraped from publicly available Instagram profiles and later cleaned and shared on Kaggle. It includes metrics such as follower count, engagement-related variables, and categorical information about the influencer’s industry or niche.

The dataset includes 200 observations, with each one representing a single high-profile Instagram account. The data comes from a specific point in the late 2010s and reflects globally recognized influencers rather than users from one particular country. One of the strengths of this dataset is that it lets us look closely at how engagement works among the most visible and followed accounts on the platform. At the same time, because it only includes the top 200 accounts, it doesn’t reflect the typical Instagram user experience. This means the sample is heavily skewed toward extremely popular influencers, who may behave very differently from everyday users on the platform.

**How These Datasets Fit the Bigger Picture**

Each dataset gives us a different perspective on Instagram. The labeled dataset helps us look at authenticity and account structure across a broader mix of users, while the top 200 dataset focuses on influencers at the highest level of visibility. But when we combine them, we’re able to compare everyday accounts with elite influencers in the same analysis. This makes it easier to see whether follower count actually reflects real influence, or if engagement and authenticity give us a more accurate picture of what influence really looks like.

# **Does Follower Count Relate to Engagement?**

**Does Follower Count Relate to Engagement?**

```{r}

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)

load("clean_combined_data.RData")

clean_combined_data <- clean_combined_data %>%
  mutate(log_followers = log10(Number.of.followers))

interactive_plot <- ggplot(clean_combined_data, aes(x = log_followers, y = engagement_potential)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Log(Followers) vs Engagement Potential",
    x = "Log10(Follower Count)",
    y = "Engagement Potential"
  )

ggplotly(interactive_plot)

```

Follower counts vary dramatically across accounts, so a log transformation was used to reduce skew and make patterns easier to see. The scatterplot allows us to examine whether engagement rate increases, decreases, or remains stable as follower count grows. If engagement declines as follower count increases, this suggests that larger audiences do not necessarily translate to proportionally higher interaction.

From the scatter plot above, we found that as follower count goes up, the engagement potential steadily declines. The more followers does not mean the more engagement to an individual's Instagram account.

# **Do Fake Accounts Differ from Authentic Accounts in terms of engagement?**

**Do Fake Accounts Differ from Authentic Accounts in terms of engagement?**

```{r}

clean_combined_data_2 <- readRDS("clean_combined_data_2.rds")
clean_combined_data <- clean_combined_data %>%
  select(-rating) %>%  
  left_join(
    clean_combined_data_2 %>% select(Username, rating),
    by = "Username"
  ) 

#table(clean_combined_data$rating, useNA = "always")

clean_combined_data <- clean_combined_data %>%
  mutate(rating = factor(as.logical(rating),
                         levels = c("TRUE", "FALSE"),
                         labels = c("Authentic", "Fake")))

ggplot(clean_combined_data, aes(x = rating, y = engagement_potential, fill = rating)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  scale_y_log10()+
  labs(
    title = "Engagement Potential by Account Authenticity",
    x = "Account Authenticity",
    y = "Engagement Potential",
  )

```

This boxplot gives a clearer sense of how engagement potential differs between authentic and fake accounts. If the two groups look very similar, however, it would imply that follower count alone isn’t enough to distinguish between real and fake accounts.

Looking at the spread and overlap between the boxes is especially important. If there’s heavy overlap, it means the difference isn’t as clear-cut as we might expect. Instead of relying on assumptions, this visualization helps us see whether meaningful differences actually appear in the data. Relatively, however, the engagement potential of fake accounts are higher than that of real accounts.

# **Are Top 200 Influencers More Engagement-Efficient?**

**Are Top 200 Influencers More Engagement-Efficient?** 

```{r}
clean_combined_data <- clean_combined_data %>%
  mutate(account_group = ifelse(rank(desc(Number.of.followers)) <= 200,
                                "Top 200 Influencers",
                                "General Account"))

engagement_summary <- clean_combined_data %>%
  group_by(account_group) %>%
  summarize(avg_engagement = mean(engagement_potential, na.rm = TRUE))

ploty1 <- ggplot(engagement_summary, aes(
      x = account_group,
      y = avg_engagement,
      fill = account_group,
       text = paste(
        "Group:", account_group,
        "<br> Average Engagement Potential:", avg_engagement
      )
)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Engagement Rate: Top 200 vs General Accounts",
    x = "Account Group",
    y = "Average Engagement Potential",
    fill = "Account Group"
  )

ggplotly(ploty1, tooltip = "text")

```

This bar chart compares the average engagement rates of the top 200 influencers with the rest of the accounts in the dataset. It helps test the assumption that the biggest accounts must also generate the strongest engagement.If the averages are similar or if top influencers actually have lower engagement rates it suggests that having a massive audience doesn’t automatically mean that followers are more interactive. The relationship between popularity and engagement appears more nuanced than it might initially seem.

However, from the graph, we see that the top 200 influencers have less of an average engagement potential compared to the normal accounts. This means that contrary to popular belief, top influencer accounts are not more engagement-efficient in relation to normal Instagram accounts.

### Coding Notes:

**Coding Notes:**

**mutate()**\
We used mutate() to create new variables, including a log-transformed follower count and grouped account categories. The raw follower numbers were extremely spread out, so transforming and grouping them made the patterns much easier to interpret.

**filter()**\
We used filter() whenever I needed to focus on a specific group of accounts. Instead of analyzing the entire dataset at once, this let us narrow things down and make clearer comparisons.

**group_by() and summarize()**

We used group_by() with summarize() to calculate average engagement rates and follower statistics across different types of accounts. This was one of the most important steps, because it allowed us to move from individual rows of data to meaningful comparisons between groups.

**ifelse()**

I used ifelse() to create a new variable that groups accounts based on follower size. This helped simplify the dataset and made it easier to compare engagement patterns between larger and smaller accounts without manually separating them.

**log() (Log Transformation)**\
We used log() to transform follower counts because the original numbers were extremely spread out. When we graphed the raw follower counts, accounts with very large audiences dominated the scale, making it hard to see patterns among smaller accounts. Applying a log transformation made the graph easier to read and allowed differences between accounts to be visible.

# Conclusion

**Conclusion**

From this assignment and our analysis of the data sets, we have established some key takeaways regarding fake influence in Instagram influencer accounts. First, a high follower count may be misleading, as it does not necessarily correspond to higher engagement or more influence. Larger accounts dominate in terms of reach, but that does not necessarily mean their metrics are proportionally higher than their followers, and so on. The log-scaled scatter plot revealed a downward trend in engagement potential as follower counts increased. This means that accounts with the largest audiences often show lower engagement relative to their account size. This suggests that influence efficiency decreases as size increases, and that raw reach is a poor indicator of real interaction. Second, the box plot comparing the engagement potential of authentic and fake accounts showed that the engagement of fake accounts were often higher than most real ones. This is consistent with inflated audience sizes and disproportionate activity. The overlap between the two groups also confirms that follower count alone is not a reliable way to identify inauthentic accounts. Third, the bar chart comparing the Top 200 influencers to general accounts showed that elite influencers do not have significantly higher average engagement potential. In some cases, they even lag behind general accounts, despite having much larger audiences. This challenges the idea that the biggest accounts are the most engaging.

Our analysis likely underestimates the true gap between authentic and inauthentic accounts. The primary dataset is sourced from a 2017 snapshot of Instagram, a period before the platform's algorithm and bot-detection systems have been updated significantly. Since then, fake account behavior has become more sophisticated and harder to detect through metrics alone. This means that the patterns we observed may actually underrepresent how distinct fake accounts look today. Additionally, our measure of engagement potential is not a direct measure of actual likes, comments, or shares, which introduces its own limitations. It's possible that the proxy smooths over more extreme differences that would be visible with raw engagement data. The "fake" classification in the dataset may also introduce measurement bias borderline accounts could be mislabeled and would weaken the observed differences between groups.

For future research, we would want to pursue a few different ideas. First, incorporating more recent data would allow us to examine how the relationship between follower count and engagement has shifted as Instagram's ecosystem evolved. Second, using direct engagement metrics such as actual like and comment counts relative to follower size would provide a more grounded and precise measure of influence efficiency than a derived potential score. Third, applying more robust fake account detection methods, such as bot-detection scores or comment sentiment analysis, would allow for a more nuanced classification beyond a simple authentic or fake binary. Finally, expanding the dataset across different industries, regions, and platform types would improve the generalizability of our findings. Ultimately, understanding what separates genuine influence from inflated popularity requires a richer, more dynamic dataset than what was available here but this analysis provides a meaningful foundation for that broader investigation.
